findings.title: Recurring event child occurrences created outside any transaction — partial failure leaves orphaned parent with no children
findings.description: After the parent event is committed (line 140–198), child occurrences are written in a sequential for-loop with N separate Prisma `event.create` calls, each in its own auto-commit transaction. If any child create fails mid-loop (e.g. DB connection reset, constraint violation on one participant, resource limit), the parent event and the already-created children are persisted but the request returns HTTP 500. The client never receives the parent ID and may retry, creating a duplicate recurrence series. Contrast with the `update()` method (lines 480–535) which correctly wraps participant rewrites in `$transaction`. The asymmetry is likely an oversight.
findings.root_cause: Occurrence loop runs as N independent writes after the parent has already committed, with no wrapping transaction to roll back on failure.
findings.impact: On any child-create failure the DB is left with a partially constructed recurrence series (parent + k < N children). A retry creates a second series from scratch. Users see duplicate events or a broken series with no API feedback beyond 500.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the parent create and all child creates in a single `this.prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => { ... })`. Move the `event.create` for the parent and the occurrence loop inside the transaction callback, substituting `tx.event.create` for all writes.
acceptance_criteria: A simulated DB error on the 2nd child create rolls back the parent and the 1st child (DB is clean after failure)
A successful call with N=3 child occurrences produces exactly 1 parent + 3 children in a single atomic write
The existing PER-024 test (event.create called parent + 2 occurrences) still passes after the refactor
findings.notes: update() wraps participant rewrite in $transaction (line 480) — the asymmetry with create() strengthens this finding.

findings.title: ICS export silently drops leaves that span the requested date window (startDate before and endDate after the range)
findings.description: The date filter for leaves uses `OR: [{startDate: dateFilter}, {endDate: dateFilter}]`. This matches leaves that START within the window or END within the window, but misses leaves that SPAN the window entirely (startDate < window.start AND endDate > window.end). A common real-world case: annual leave running Jan 1–Dec 31, exported for June 1–30 — neither condition matches and the leave is absent from the ICS. Events and telework days use a point-in-time `date` field so they are unaffected. Only the leave filter has this semantic gap.
findings.root_cause: The OR filter checks that startDate falls in the range OR endDate falls in the range, but never checks for spanning overlap (startDate <= end AND endDate >= start).
findings.impact: Any approved leave whose full duration spans the export window is silently omitted from the calendar export. Users relying on ICS sync to their calendar app will see gaps — particularly for long continuous leaves (parental leave, extended sick leave). No error is raised; the export appears successful.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the OR filter with an overlap predicate: `{ startDate: { lte: new Date(end) }, endDate: { gte: new Date(start) } }`. This is the standard "intervals overlap" condition and matches all three cases (leave starting in range, ending in range, or spanning).
acceptance_criteria: An APPROVED leave with startDate='2026-01-01' and endDate='2026-12-31' is included in an ICS export requested for start='2026-06-01' end='2026-06-30'
A leave that starts and ends within the window is still included
A leave entirely outside the window is still excluded
findings.notes: Events and telework use a single date point so they are unaffected. Only the leaves query has this spanning-overlap gap.

findings.title: addParticipant uses check-then-create pattern susceptible to race; duplicate concurrent request leaks P2002 as HTTP 500
findings.description: Two concurrent requests to add the same (eventId, userId) pair both pass the findUnique existence check before either write commits. The second create hits the DB-level unique constraint `eventId_userId` and Prisma raises P2002, which is unhandled and surfaces as HTTP 500 instead of the intended 400 BadRequestException. DB integrity is maintained; only the error code is wrong. The `update()` path avoids this by wrapping inside a transaction.
findings.root_cause: Check-then-act pattern without unique-constraint error handling; concurrent requests can both pass the guard before the DB serializes them.
findings.impact: Under concurrent load (unlikely but possible), a duplicate-add request returns 500 instead of 400, misleading clients into treating a constraint violation as a server fault.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the create in a try/catch and map Prisma P2002 on `eventId_userId` to `BadRequestException('Cet utilisateur est déjà participant')`, or use `createMany` with `skipDuplicates: true`.
acceptance_criteria: Concurrent duplicate addParticipant calls both return 400 (not 500)
Unit test: mock prisma.eventParticipant.create to throw a Prisma P2002 error; service throws BadRequestException
findings.notes: DB integrity is safe due to the unique constraint. Only the error response code is affected in the concurrent scenario.

findings.title: update() recurrenceEndDate child-prune runs outside the update transaction, creating a window where pruned children exist alongside a committed new endDate
findings.description: The parent event is updated inside `$transaction` (lines 480–535) which commits before this code runs. The child-prune `deleteMany` at lines 551–556 executes as a separate independent write. If the server crashes or is killed between the transaction commit and this write, the DB is left with a parent whose `recurrenceEndDate` has been shortened but whose out-of-range children still exist. Additionally, shortening an end date prunes children but extending it never regenerates them, which may be intentional by design but is undocumented.
findings.root_cause: Child pruning is implemented as a post-transaction side-effect rather than being included inside the $transaction scope at lines 480–535.
findings.impact: On crash between the transaction commit and the deleteMany, stale child events persist beyond the new recurrenceEndDate. The invariant 'no child.date > parent.recurrenceEndDate' is temporarily violated. Planners relying on the planning view may see ghost occurrences after a recurrence is shortened.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the child `deleteMany` inside the `$transaction` callback (before or after the `tx.event.update`) so all changes are atomic.
acceptance_criteria: Simulated crash between update and deleteMany leaves no stale children after recovery
The child prune and parent update either both commit or both roll back
findings.notes: Extending recurrenceEndDate never regenerates children; this is a separate limitation (not filed as a separate finding — insufficient confidence that this is unintentional vs. out-of-scope).

verified_clean: planning/planning.service.ts — getOverview() two-wave Promise.all: pure read aggregation, partial failure is acceptable (returns 500 to caller, no writes involved)
planning/planning.service.ts — dateOnlyStart/dateOnlyEnd slicing (slice(0,10)) correctly truncates ISO8601 timestamps to YYYY-MM-DD for services expecting date-only strings
planning/planning.service.ts — leavesResult dual-shape handling (Array.isArray guard) correctly covers both paginated and raw-array return shapes from LeavesService.findAll
planning/planning.service.ts — canViewPredefinedTasks permission gate correctly resolves to Promise.resolve([]) when user lacks the permission
events/events.service.ts — update() participant rewrite is correctly wrapped in $transaction (lines 480–535) with deleteMany + createMany inside the callback
events/events.service.ts — isEventParentCycleViolation helper correctly covers both P0001 (trigger path) and 23514 CHECK constraint path with AND of two signals to avoid false positives
events/events.service.ts — findAll() RBAC scoping: non-readAll users are scoped to events they created or participate in via OR clause; admin/readAll path allows userId filter
events/events.service.ts — findOne() IDOR check: users without events:readAll cannot access events they neither created nor participate in (ForbiddenException)
events/events.service.ts — stopRecurrence() correctly guards against child events (parentEventId non-null) and non-recurring events before executing deleteMany+update
events/events.service.ts — stopRecurrence() uses today.setHours(0,0,0,0) which is local-midnight; on Prod=UTC this is equivalent to UTC midnight — acceptable
planning-export/planning-export.service.ts — fire-and-forget audit log correctly uses void + .catch() to avoid propagating audit failures to the export response
planning-export/planning-export.service.ts — importIcs swallowed catch (line 264) is intentional resilience (per-event skip-on-error); confirmed by spec asserting skip-on-create-error
planning-export/planning-export.service.ts — exportIcs timed event local setHours math (lines 77-86): Prod=UTC neutralizes DST/offset drift; not a practical issue
planning-export/planning-export.service.ts — events and teleworkDays date filter uses single date point so no spanning-overlap gap (only leaves are affected by S7-2)
events/dto/create-event.dto.ts — recurrenceDay validated with @IsInt @Min(0) @Max(6), @IsOptional — validation is structurally correct even if the stored value is not applied in the occurrence loop
planning/dto/planning-overview-query.dto.ts — @IsISO8601 + @IsNotEmpty correctly validates both startDate and endDate
planning-export/dto/import-ics.dto.ts — @MaxLength(5 * 1024 * 1024) caps import payload size appropriately
